claims.evidence_text: the synthesis-analysis portrayed new insights of differences in AOPD at varying YEs, reducing the AOPD as the attainable yield increases (with AOPD moving from 397 pl m -2 for the low YE to 191 pl m -2 for the high YE)
claims.claim_summary: Agronomic optimum plant density for winter wheat ranges ~141-397 plants/m2, decreasing as yield environment increases (397 low YE, 297 medium, 141 high YE).

claims.evidence_text: five winter wheat genotypes sown at five target seeding rates (150, 235, 321, 408, 494 seeds m -2 )
claims.claim_summary: Winter wheat field trials used target seeding rates of 150-494 seeds/m2; producers advised 300-350 seeds/m2 for low-medium yield environments.

claims.evidence_text: Each experimental unit was 10 m long by seven rows (0.19-m spaced) except for two site-years (Hutchinson 2015-2016 and Hays 2017-2018) that consisted of six rows (0.25-m spaced).
claims.claim_summary: Winter wheat rows spaced 0.19-0.25 m apart in Kansas field trials.

claims.evidence_text: The thousand-kernel weight was not significantly affected by any of the explanatory variables and varied from 11.8 to 36 g
claims.claim_summary: Thousand-kernel weight in winter wheat ranged 11.8-36 g, not significantly affected by plant density, yield environment or tillering potential.

claims.evidence_text: kernels per head was greatest at the <100 PD group (c.a., 26.4), and lowest when PD group >300 (c.a., 20.7 to 21.2)
claims.claim_summary: Kernels per head (grains per spike) in winter wheat ranged ~20.7-26.4, decreasing with increasing plant density.

claims.evidence_text: Averaged across YE and TP, heads m -2 increased from 680 to 893 as PD group increased from <100 to >400, respectively.
claims.claim_summary: Head (spike) density in winter wheat ranged 680-893 heads/m2, increasing with plant density.

claims.evidence_text: Considering a radiation use efficiency of 1.4 g MJ -1 and a harvest index of 0.4 ( Lollato and Edwards, 2015 )
claims.claim_summary: Typical winter wheat harvest index of 0.4 used for yield potential calculations.

claims.evidence_text: Considering a radiation use efficiency of 1.4 g MJ -1 and a harvest index of 0.4 ( Lollato and Edwards, 2015 )
claims.claim_summary: Winter wheat radiation use efficiency of 1.4 g/MJ used for yield potential estimation.

claims.evidence_text: fractional green canopy cover was as high as 82% in the medium YE but never surpassed 67% in the low YE
claims.claim_summary: Fractional green canopy cover in winter wheat peaked at 67% (low YE) to 82% (medium YE).

claims.evidence_text: Grain moisture content was measured at harvest time and yields corrected for 130 g kg -1 moisture content.
claims.claim_summary: Winter wheat grain yields corrected to 130 g/kg (13%) moisture content at harvest.

claims.evidence_text: Diammonium phosphate (18-46-0) was applied in-furrow at sowing at a rate of 55 kg ha -1 .
claims.claim_summary: Diammonium phosphate (18-46-0) applied in-furrow at 55 kg/ha at sowing for winter wheat.

claims.evidence_text: Nitrogen fertilizer was applied as urea (46-0-0) during early spring in rates sufficient to meet a yield goal of 4.7 Mg ha -1
claims.claim_summary: Nitrogen applied as urea in early spring for winter wheat to meet yield goal.

claims.evidence_text: while lodging is recognized as a reoccurring issue in wheat grown under high populations ... it ranged from non-existing to moderate in most of the site-years studied
claims.claim_summary: Lodging is a recurring issue in wheat grown under high plant populations.
